<findings>

[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.67) — 25 imports target "prd-operations-core"
[observation] [warning] Low cohesion (0.33) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
[observation] [warning] 12 imports from unit-cli into the broader cli zone and 10 reverse imports from cli into unit-cli indicate the boundary between these two CLI zones is not crisp — consider merging or more clearly separating concerns.
[observation] [warning] Cohesion of 0.33 and coupling of 0.67 flag this zone as a coordination layer rather than a cohesive domain — expected for a CLI command tier, but worth monitoring as commands are added.
[observation] [info] cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts as entry points are healthy signs — centralized error and validation handling prevents inconsistent user-facing messages across commands.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "mutation-commands" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [warning] cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts belong to all CLI commands equally but are zone-captured by mutation-commands. Extract them into a dedicated src/cli/infrastructure/ directory and assign them to a shared cli-infrastructure zone, making their cross-cutting role explicit and eliminating accidental zone-boundary crossings from infrastructure imports.
[suggestion] [info] src/cli/commands/recommend.ts is classified as [cli-command] but is zone-assigned to remote-integration because it imports from acknowledge.ts — the zone algorithm followed import edges rather than the file's architectural role. A misclassified zone assignment for a CLI command module means coupling metrics for both remote-integration and mutation-commands are distorted: remote-integration appears to have CLI presence, and mutation-commands appears to lack it.
[suggestion] [warning] Zone "Mutation Commands" (mutation-commands) has critical risk (score: 0.67, cohesion: 0.33, coupling: 0.67) — requires refactoring before new feature development
[relationship] [warning] 14 imports from unit-analyze into unit-cli (mutation-commands consuming analysis pipeline internals) create a layering violation: the command layer should call high-level service functions, not import from the analysis pipeline directly.
[relationship] [warning] The 22 mutual imports between mutation-commands and the cli zone indicate they are effectively one zone with an arbitrary split boundary; the high bidirectional traffic should trigger a deliberate merge-or-separate decision rather than continued organic growth.
[anti-pattern] [info] cli/help.ts is misclassified as [types] despite containing runtime formatting logic and an @n-dx/llm-client dependency. Archetype should be [utility] or [service] — the wrong classification distorts coupling metrics for this zone and may suppress findings about the cross-package dependency.
[anti-pattern] [warning] src/cli/index.ts (the CLI entry point) is zone-assigned to mutation-commands rather than to a dedicated entrypoint or orchestration zone. This couples the neutral entry point to the mutation-command zone boundary, making future extraction of mutation commands into a sub-package harder.

</findings>

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.33) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.67) — 25 imports target "prd-operations-core"
- This zone owns the write path of the CLI — every user-facing mutation command lives here, making it the right place to enforce input validation and consistent error messaging.
- Coupling of 0.67 reflects that mutation commands depend on both core domain logic and the analyze pipeline, but contribute little back — this is architecturally correct for a command layer.
- parent-completion.ts, parent-reset.ts, and timestamps.ts appearing as entry points suggests lifecycle side-effects are spread across multiple small modules; consolidating them into a single parent-lifecycle.ts would reduce surface area.
- Cohesion of 0.33 and coupling of 0.67 flag this zone as a coordination layer rather than a cohesive domain — expected for a CLI command tier, but worth monitoring as commands are added.
- 12 imports from unit-cli into the broader cli zone and 10 reverse imports from cli into unit-cli indicate the boundary between these two CLI zones is not crisp — consider merging or more clearly separating concerns.
- cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts as entry points are healthy signs — centralized error and validation handling prevents inconsistent user-facing messages across commands.
- Zone "mutation-commands" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- mutation-commands depends on both core (domain primitives) and prd-analysis-pipeline (14 imports from unit-analyze into unit-cli) creating a diamond dependency: mutation commands reach through two layers to consume analysis internals, which should instead be mediated by a command-layer service or facade.
- The 12 mutual imports between mutation-commands and the separate cli zone (10 cli→unit-cli, 12 unit-cli→cli) indicate the two CLI zones share internal helpers without a declared interface — each zone is importing from the other's internals rather than through a published API.
- 14 imports from unit-analyze into unit-cli (mutation-commands consuming analysis pipeline internals) create a layering violation: the command layer should call high-level service functions, not import from the analysis pipeline directly.
- The 22 mutual imports between mutation-commands and the cli zone indicate they are effectively one zone with an arbitrary split boundary; the high bidirectional traffic should trigger a deliberate merge-or-separate decision rather than continued organic growth.
- cli/help.ts is classified as [types] by the zone analyzer but contains runtime behavior: it imports formatHelp() from @n-dx/llm-client, builds COMMAND_DEFS records, and executes formatting logic. The misclassification causes the zone to under-report its runtime dependency on @n-dx/llm-client.
- cli/index.ts (the CLI entry point) resides inside the mutation-commands zone — the entry point's zone placement means changing or testing the entry point requires reasoning about mutation-command-specific zone membership rather than a neutral orchestration boundary.
- cli/help.ts is misclassified as [types] despite containing runtime formatting logic and an @n-dx/llm-client dependency. Archetype should be [utility] or [service] — the wrong classification distorts coupling metrics for this zone and may suppress findings about the cross-package dependency.
- src/cli/index.ts (the CLI entry point) is zone-assigned to mutation-commands rather than to a dedicated entrypoint or orchestration zone. This couples the neutral entry point to the mutation-command zone boundary, making future extraction of mutation commands into a sub-package harder.
- cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts are infrastructure utilities shared by all CLI commands, but their zone membership in mutation-commands means any CLI zone that imports them crosses a zone boundary. This creates accidental coupling from infrastructure-sharing: the more CLI commands grow, the more zone-boundary crossings accumulate from shared error and validation infrastructure rather than from genuine cross-domain logic.
- src/recommend/ (3 files: types.ts, conflict-detection.ts, create-from-recommendations.ts) is a minimal three-file directory that earned its own zone designation. Its small size and the fact that recommend.ts CLI command imports from it directly suggests src/recommend/ is a satellite directory created to house analysis-adjacent logic that didn't fit analyze/ — a symptom of the same catch-all pattern as src/workflow/ and the misplaced acknowledge.ts.
- cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts belong to all CLI commands equally but are zone-captured by mutation-commands. Extract them into a dedicated src/cli/infrastructure/ directory and assign them to a shared cli-infrastructure zone, making their cross-cutting role explicit and eliminating accidental zone-boundary crossings from infrastructure imports.
- src/cli/commands/recommend.ts is classified as [cli-command] but is zone-assigned to remote-integration because it imports from acknowledge.ts — the zone algorithm followed import edges rather than the file's architectural role. A misclassified zone assignment for a CLI command module means coupling metrics for both remote-integration and mutation-commands are distorted: remote-integration appears to have CLI presence, and mutation-commands appears to lack it.
- [call graph] 569 internal calls, 85 outgoing, 409 incoming (cohesion: 0.87, coupling: 0.13)

</insights>
